nuggets.headline: Radiohead were pioneers of the internet, building their online presence decades ago
nuggets.text: Before social media made every artist accessible, Radiohead were already connecting with fans online. In 1997, when less than one percent of the world had web access, they were one of the first bands to launch an official website, albeit a sparse and cryptic one. This early digital hub laid the groundwork for the intensely dedicated online community that still scrutinizes every rumored move the band makes today. Their foresight into the technological age was so deep, frontman Thom Yorke sang about the experience of using a message board on a track from their 1997 album, *OK Computer*.

source.title: Internet Explorers: The Curious Case of Radiohead’s Online Fandom | Pitchfork
source.quoteSnippet: Radiohead isn’t the only band whose mere gestures get turned into news stories, of course, but they’re unique in that the underlying architecture of their internet fan network has been in place for more than 20 years. As the internet developed, so did Radiohead… 20 years ago, though, most bands didn’t even have a website. Radiohead were an exception.

nuggets.imageCaption: Radiohead members in a black and white portrait.

nuggets.headline: Radiohead's *In Rainbows* pioneered the 'pay-what-you-want' album model
nuggets.text: When *In Rainbows* was released in 2007, Radiohead turned the music industry on its head by offering the digital download via a revolutionary 'pay-what-you-want' model. This bold move challenged traditional distribution norms at a time when digital music was flourishing but industry models were crumbling. The band was so meticulous, they spent six hours debating the optimal bit rate for the downloaded files, aiming for a quality better than iTunes but not quite CD-level. This attention to detail ensured that even their groundbreaking business decision was backed by a commitment to immaculate sound.

source.title: Album Review: Radiohead’s In Rainbows - Tune Tempest
source.quoteSnippet: Radiohead’s decision to release the album via a pay-what-you-want model was revolutionary, challenging conventional distribution norms and asserting the band’s commitment to artistry over commerce.

nuggets.headline: if 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' is hitting right now, you need to hear Atoms for Peace
nuggets.text: If you're digging this track, you absolutely need to dive into Atoms for Peace. This experimental rock and electronic supergroup was formed in 2009 and features Radiohead's own frontman, Thom Yorke, on vocals, guitars, and piano, alongside Red Hot Chili Peppers bassist Flea and longtime Radiohead producer Nigel Godrich. It's a direct lineage to more of Yorke's unique melodic and rhythmic sensibilities outside of Radiohead, exploring glitchy electronic sounds and intricate grooves.

source.title: Similar artists - Thom Yorke | Last.fm
source.quoteSnippet: Atoms for Peace is an experimental rock and electronic group formed in late 2009 in Los Angeles, California, United States. The group consists of Radiohead lead singer Thom Yorke (vocals, guitars, and piano), Red Hot Chili Peppers bassist Flea (bass guitar), longtime Radiohead producer Nigel Godrich

nuggets.imageCaption: Atoms for Peace members on stage.

artistSummary: Radiohead are a British alternative rock band formed in the mid-1980s, known for their innovative sound and deeply devoted fanbase. Comprising Thom Yorke, Jonny Greenwood, Colin Greenwood, Ed O'Brien, and Philip Selway, they've consistently pushed genre boundaries, blending rock, electronica, jazz, and classical elements across their acclaimed discography.
